source: Generating Alpha — Ep.56: career arc, semis, NVDA/Tesla, AI infra cycles
summary: Watts and wafers shortage may prevent an AI overbuild/bubble, alongside the psychological scars of the dot-com bubble
summary_extended: Baker argues that two structural physical constraints — a shortage of power (watts) available for data center buildout and a shortage of semiconductor manufacturing capacity (wafers, primarily at TSMC) — will act as natural governors preventing the AI infrastructure cycle from becoming a true bubble or overbuild. Unlike the dot-com era, where capital could flow freely into fiber and networking buildout, the AI cycle is gated by real-world physical limits that cannot be quickly resolved regardless of capital availability. Baker also cites the psychological scarring of dot-com-era investors as an additional behavioral brake on excess speculation. The investment implication is that AI infrastructure spending is more likely to be supply-constrained than demand-constrained, meaning the cycle could be longer and more durable than skeptics expect, with less risk of a catastrophic overbuild collapse. No specific price targets or timeframes are cited, but Baker frames this as a medium-term structural view he holds with high conviction.
detail: Baker argues two structural constraints — physical shortage of power (watts) for data centers and semiconductor manufacturing capacity (wafers/TSMC) — combined with investor memory of the dot-com bust, will likely prevent a true AI bubble and overbuild cycle.
confidence_evidence: Repeated multiple times as a core structural thesis; Baker says he is 'optimistic and hopeful' this prevents a bubble
quote_fragment: fundamentally short watts and wafers and no prior...that shortage may prevent an overbuild

ticker_audit.reasoning: TSMC is the primary wafer capacity constraint Baker references — the thesis is explicitly about TSMC's manufacturing capacity as a structural bottleneck for AI infrastructure, making it a direct subject of the thesis and a direct beneficiary of sustained, non-overbuild demand.
